merger_name: Metcash - Foodland Flagstaff Hill, South Australia
merger_description:
Action Supermarkets Pty Ltd, a subsidiary of Metcash Limited (together, **Metcash**), proposes to acquire all of the business and assets of ZH Supermarket Pty Ltd, an independent supermarket trading as Foodland Flagstaff Hill in South Australia (**Foodland Flagstaff Hill**).

Metcash is an ASX-listed wholesaler and distributor of food, liquor and hardware products. Metcash’s supermarkets division supplies packaged groceries and fresh produce to a network of around 2,500 independently owned supermarket stores Australia-wide, including to its banner groups IGA and Foodland. Metcash provides its banner groups with brand licencing, marketing and promotional support. In addition, Metcash also services non-bannered stores and third-party groups such as FoodWorks.

Foodland Flagstaff Hill is an independent supermarket operating under the Foodland banner and is a wholesale customer of Metcash. It is located in the Flagstaff Hill Shopping Centre Precinct, approximately 18 kilometres south of the Adelaide central business district.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Action Supermarkets Pty Ltd, a subsidiary of Metcash Limited
(ASX:MTS) (Metcash), applied for a notification waiver in respect of
its proposed acquisition of the business and assets of ZH
Supermarket Pty Ltd (Foodland Flagstaff Hill), as described in the
transaction documents provided as part of the application (the
Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, Metcash, is an ASX-listed wholesaler and distributor of
food, liquor and hardware products. Metcash’s supermarkets
division supplies packaged groceries and fresh produce to a network
of around 2,500 independently owned supermarket stores Australia-
wide, including to its banner groups IGA and Foodland. Metcash
provides its banner groups with brand licensing, marketing and
promotional support. Metcash also services non-bannered stores
and third-party groups such as FoodWorks.
In addition to its wholesale operations, Metcash currently operates 3
retail supermarkets in Australia – an IGA store in Turramurra NSW,
and 2 Foodland stores in Goolwa and Naracoorte in South Australia.
Metcash’s supermarkets in South Australia are located
approximately 70km and 338km away from Foodland Flagstaff Hill,
respectively.
The target, Foodland Flagstaff Hill, is an independent supermarket
operating under the Foodland banner and is a wholesale customer of
Metcash. It is located in the Flagstaff Hill Shopping Centre Precinct,
approximately 18 kilometres south of the Adelaide central business
district.
There is a vertical relationship between the parties as Metcash
supplies wholesale grocery products to Foodland Flagstaff Hill.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. there is no horizontal overlap between Metcash and
Foodland Flagstaff Hill in the retail supply of groceries in the
Flagstaff Hill and surrounding areas
b. there are alternative retail supermarkets in Flagstaff Hill and
surrounding areas
c. there is a low risk of foreclosure or other concerning vertical
effects resulting from exclusionary conduct post-acquisition.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.
